longTailKeywords: hepatitis A vs hepatitis B acute

h1Title: Acute Viral Hepatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute viral hepatitis is sudden-onset liver inflammation caused by one of five primary hepatitis viruses — A, B, C, D, or E — each with a distinct transmission route, natural history, and risk of chronicity. The World Health Organization estimates 354 million people live with chronic hepatitis B or C, but tens of millions more experience acute infections annually. Most acute episodes produce jaundice, fatigue, nausea, and elevated liver enzymes and resolve within 4-12 weeks; however, hepatitis B and C can transition to chronic infection in 5-90% of cases depending on age and immunity, and acute liver failure, though rare, carries mortality above 50% without transplantation. Effective vaccines prevent hepatitis A and B entirely; direct-acting antivirals cure more than 95% of chronic hepatitis C.

keyStats.prevalence: WHO estimates 296 million people with chronic hepatitis B and 58 million with chronic hepatitis C globally (2019); acute hepatitis A infects approximately 1.4 million persons per year
keyStats.demographics: Hepatitis A and E predominate in low-income regions with poor sanitation; hepatitis B peaks in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia; hepatitis C disproportionately affects people who inject drugs and recipients of blood products before 1990
keyStats.avgAge: Hepatitis A and E most common in children and young adults in endemic regions; perinatal hepatitis B infection peaks in infancy; hepatitis C drug-use-related peaks age 20-40
keyStats.globalCases: Viral hepatitis caused approximately 1.1 million deaths in 2019 (WHO), predominantly from hepatitis B (820,000) and hepatitis C (290,000) complications

definition: Acute viral hepatitis (ICD-10: B17.9) is inflammation of hepatocytes developing within the first six months of infection by a hepatotropic virus. Five distinct viruses account for most cases: hepatitis A virus (HAV, picornavirus, fecal-oral transmission, self-limiting); hepatitis B virus (HBV, hepadnavirus, bloodborne and sexual transmission, 5-10% chronicity in adults); hepatitis C virus (HCV, flavivirus, bloodborne transmission, 55-85% chronicity); hepatitis D virus (HDV, delta agent, requires HBV co-infection, bloodborne); and hepatitis E virus (HEV, hepevirus, primarily fecal-oral, self-limiting except in pregnant women and immunocompromised hosts). Each virus targets hepatocytes via specific surface receptors, and liver damage results predominantly from the host cytotoxic T-lymphocyte response rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Histologically, acute hepatitis shows lobular disarray, hepatocyte ballooning and necrosis, lymphocytic infiltration, and, in severe cases, bridging or massive necrosis leading to acute liver failure. Serum transaminases (ALT, AST) rise markedly — typically 10-100 times the upper limit of normal — reflecting hepatocyte membrane disruption. Gastroenterology and hepatology specialists manage complicated cases; infectious disease specialists guide management of co-infections and public-health aspects.

typesClassification.description: Transmitted via contaminated food and water. Self-limiting in 99% of cases; recovery within 4-8 weeks. No chronicity. Fulminant hepatic failure occurs in less than 0.5% but risk rises with underlying liver disease. Prevention: inactivated HAV vaccine, highly effective after 2 doses.

Transmitted via blood, sexual contact, and vertical (mother-to-child) transmission. Incubation 1-6 months. Chronicity in 5-10% of immunocompetent adults, 90% of perinatally infected neonates. Fulminant hepatitis in 0.5-1%. Prevention: HBV vaccine; treatment: tenofovir or entecavir for those who do not clear the virus.

Transmitted primarily via blood (injecting drug use, unsterile procedures). Incubation 2-24 weeks; 80% of acute infections are asymptomatic. Chronic infection in 55-85%; end-stage liver disease and hepatocellular carcinoma follow decades later. No vaccine available; direct-acting antivirals cure >95%.

A defective RNA virus requiring simultaneous HBV infection (co-infection) or HDV superinfection of chronic HBV. Accelerates liver damage; co-infection with HBV resolves in most cases; superinfection progresses to chronicity in 70-90%. Prevention by HBV vaccination effectively prevents HDV.

Predominantly fecal-oral transmission via contaminated water (genotypes 1 and 2 in Asia and Africa); zoonotic transmission via undercooked pork (genotypes 3 and 4 in industrialized countries). Self-limiting in most adults, but carries 15-25% maternal mortality in pregnant women in the third trimester and can become chronic in organ-transplant recipients.

primarySymptoms: Jaundice — visible yellowing of the skin and sclerae appearing 3-10 days after onset, caused by impaired hepatic bilirubin conjugation and excretion; bilirubin levels typically 2-20 mg/dL.
Profound fatigue, lethargy, and anorexia that often precede jaundice by 1-2 weeks and may persist for weeks after transaminases normalize.
Nausea, vomiting, and right upper-quadrant or epigastric abdominal pain from capsular stretching of the enlarged, tender liver.
Dark urine resembling strong tea or cola before jaundice appears, caused by bilirubinuria from conjugated bilirubin spillover into urine.
Pale or clay-colored stools from reduced or absent urobilinogen when intrahepatic cholestasis develops.
Low-grade fever (37.5-38.5°C) and generalized malaise during the prodromal phase, more prominent in hepatitis A and E than in HBV or HCV.
Arthralgia and urticaria in serum-sickness-like prodrome of acute hepatitis B, caused by circulating immune complexes; resolves once jaundice appears.
Weight loss of 2-4 kg from anorexia and nausea during the acute icteric phase.
Pruritus from bile-salt accumulation in the skin during cholestatic phase, particularly in hepatitis A and elderly patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Profound anorexia and aversion to alcohol and cigarettes in a smoker or drinker — one of the earliest prodromal symptoms, appearing 1-2 weeks before jaundice.
Dark urine appearing before any visible yellowing of the skin — often the first objective abnormality noticed by patients.
Unexplained ALT rise (more than three times normal) on routine blood tests without clear cause in a person with risk factors for hepatitis A, B, C, or E.
New onset of fatigue and nausea in a person who recently returned from endemic travel, received a blood product, or started injecting drugs.
emergencySigns: Acute liver failure: deep jaundice with coagulopathy (INR >1.5) and hepatic encephalopathy (confusion, drowsiness, asterixis) — requires urgent transfer to a liver transplant center.
Ascites or peripheral edema developing within weeks of onset in an acute setting — suggests acute-on-chronic liver failure or severe hepatic decompensation.
Hypoglycemia with altered mental status in the setting of acute liver failure — the liver can no longer maintain blood glucose; requires intravenous dextrose and ICU care.
Gastrointestinal bleeding (hematemesis, melena) in a patient with prolonged prothrombin time — indicates coagulopathy from hepatic synthetic failure.

causes.cause: Hepatitis A virus (HAV) via fecal-oral route
causes.description: HAV is ingested in contaminated food or water; virus replicates in intestinal epithelium and reaches the liver via the portal circulation. Fecal shedding peaks 1-2 weeks before jaundice and continues for 1-3 weeks after. Shellfish harvested from sewage-contaminated water, raw vegetables fertilized with human waste, and person-to-person contact in households and daycare settings are the main vectors.

causes.cause: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) via blood, sexual contact, or vertical transmission
causes.description: HBV is 50-100 times more infectious per milliliter of blood than HIV. Horizontal transmission occurs via unprotected sexual contact, needle sharing, tattooing with unsterile equipment, and occupational needlestick injuries. Vertical (perinatal) transmission from HBsAg-positive mothers accounts for 50% of the global chronic HBV burden.

causes.cause: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) via blood exposure
causes.description: Injecting drug use with shared needles accounts for 70-80% of new HCV infections in high-income countries. Blood transfusions before universal screening (pre-1990 in most countries), unsafe injection practices in healthcare, and perinatal transmission are other routes. Sexual transmission is uncommon except in HIV-co-infected men who have sex with men.

causes.cause: Hepatitis E virus (HEV) via contaminated water or undercooked pork
causes.description: In low- and middle-income countries, HEV genotype 1 and 2 spread via sewage-contaminated drinking water. In high-income countries, genotype 3 and 4 are zoonotic, acquired from undercooked pork, wild game, or organ meats. HEV superinfection in patients with pre-existing liver disease causes disproportionately severe acute-on-chronic liver failure.

causes.cause: Hepatitis D virus (HDV) co-infection or superinfection with HBV
causes.description: HDV requires HBV surface antigen for replication. Co-infection (simultaneous HBV and HDV) typically resolves, though fulminant hepatitis risk is higher than with HBV alone. Superinfection of a chronic HBV carrier with HDV carries 70-90% risk of chronic HDV and accelerated fibrosis to cirrhosis within 5-10 years.

Acute hepatitis damage is largely immune-mediated: cytotoxic CD8+ T-lymphocytes recognize viral antigens on infected hepatocytes and trigger apoptosis and necrosis. A vigorous immune response clears the virus but causes severe transaminase elevation; an inadequate response fails to clear the virus but may produce minimal acute symptoms while establishing chronicity.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in hepatitis A or E endemic regions
riskFactors.description: South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Central America have high HAV endemicity. HEV genotype 1 and 2 epidemics occur in displacement camps and post-flood communities with collapsed water infrastructure.

riskFactors.factor: Injecting drug use with shared equipment
riskFactors.description: The dominant route of new HCV infections in high-income countries; 70-80% of people who inject drugs in the US are HCV-antibody positive (CDC). Needle exchange programs reduce transmission by 40-60%.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sexual contact with an HBV-positive partner
riskFactors.description: HBV transmission probability per unprotected sex act is 10-30× higher than HIV. Men who have sex with men and people with multiple sexual partners are at highest risk; barrier contraception and vaccination prevent transmission.

Neonates born to HBsAg-positive mothers have a 70-90% risk of HBV infection without prophylaxis; all should receive HBV vaccine plus immunoglobulin within 12 hours of birth. HCV perinatal transmission rate is 4-8%.

riskFactors.factor: Healthcare or occupational exposure to blood
riskFactors.description: Needlestick injuries carry a 6-30% HBV transmission risk in unvaccinated individuals versus 0.3% for HIV. Universal precautions and HBV vaccination of healthcare workers are mandated in most countries.
Superimposed acute hepatitis A or E on compensated cirrhosis from any cause carries 40-70% mortality from acute-on-chronic liver failure — far higher than in healthy individuals.

HEV genotype 1 infection in the third trimester of pregnancy causes fulminant hepatic failure in 15-25% of affected women, with maternal mortality from coagulopathy and encephalopathy. The mechanism involves pregnancy-specific immune tolerance changes.

affectedDemographics: Hepatitis A primarily affects children in endemic regions and unvaccinated adults in high-income countries following travel or outbreak exposure; seroprevalence below 20% in children in the US and Western Europe after vaccine introduction.

Hepatitis B: highest burden in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia (HBsAg prevalence 5-10% of adults); in high-income countries, concentrated in migrants from endemic regions, people who inject drugs, and men who have sex with men.
Hepatitis C: estimated 58 million people globally; disproportionate burden in people who inject drugs, incarcerated populations, and recipients of unscreened blood products before 1990.
Hepatitis E: major cause of epidemic jaundice in South Asia, East Africa, and conflict or disaster zones; isolated sporadic cases in pig-farming communities in high-income countries.
Children and adolescents in endemic HBV regions acquire infection perinatally or in early childhood; 90% of neonatal infections become chronic without vaccination and immunoglobulin prophylaxis.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of acute viral hepatitis starts with serum liver function tests (transaminases, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, GGT) and a coagulation panel. In acute hepatitis, ALT and AST are typically 10-100 times the upper limit of normal, with a pattern of hepatocellular rather than cholestatic injury. Once biochemically confirmed, the causative virus is identified by serology and, where needed, NAT (nucleic acid testing). Acute hepatitis A is confirmed by anti-HAV IgM. Acute hepatitis B requires anti-HBc IgM (the most reliable marker of acute infection), HBsAg, HBeAg, and serum HBV DNA; resolution is marked by clearance of HBsAg and appearance of anti-HBs. Acute hepatitis C is identified by anti-HCV antibody (may be negative in the first 8-12 weeks) confirmed by HCV RNA PCR, which is positive from week 2. Acute hepatitis E is confirmed by anti-HEV IgM and, in immunocompromised patients, HEV RNA in serum or stool. Hepatitis D requires anti-HDV antibody and HDV RNA. Liver biopsy is rarely needed for acute hepatitis but guides prognosis in cases where fulminant liver failure or an uncertain chronicity is suspected. Abdominal ultrasound excludes biliary obstruction, assesses liver size and echogenicity, and identifies ascites or varices suggesting pre-existing cirrhosis. Prothrombin time (PT) and serum albumin quantify hepatic synthetic function; an INR above 1.5 with encephalopathy defines acute liver failure and warrants urgent transplant evaluation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify hepatocyte injury; ALT is more liver-specific and typically higher than AST in viral hepatitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results available within 1-2 hours. Values 10-100× above normal in acute viral hepatitis; normalization over 4-12 weeks signals resolution.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific hepatitis virus: anti-HAV IgM (hepatitis A), anti-HBc IgM + HBsAg (acute hepatitis B), anti-HCV + HCV RNA (hepatitis C), anti-HEV IgM (hepatitis E)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 24-72 hours in most hospital labs. If anti-HCV is negative but HCV is clinically suspected within the first 8 weeks, HCV RNA PCR is ordered independently.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures viral load in hepatitis B; determines whether infection is active and guides treatment decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; specialized PCR test returning within 2-5 days. High viral loads (>200,000 IU/mL) in acute HBV are associated with more severe disease and higher chronicity risk in some settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses hepatic synthetic function; INR >1.5 in the context of encephalopathy defines acute liver failure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 30-60 minutes. Serial measurement every 24-48 hours in hospitalized patients tracks trajectory toward or away from liver failure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes biliary obstruction mimicking hepatitis, assesses liver size, echogenicity, splenomegaly, and identifies ascites or portal hypertension suggesting pre-existing cirrhosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute non-invasive scan with gel on the abdomen. No radiation. Results available immediately; formal report within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cholestasis; markedly elevated direct bilirubin explains jaundice, dark urine, and pale stools
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the standard liver panel blood draw. Total bilirubin above 2-3 mg/dL produces visible jaundice.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is largely supportive for self-limiting forms of acute viral hepatitis (HAV, HEV, and most acute HBV in immunocompetent adults). Rest, adequate hydration, and nutritional support form the backbone of management. All hepatotoxic medications — acetaminophen in doses above 2 g/day, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, herbal hepatotoxins, and alcohol — must be stopped immediately. Hospitalization is indicated for patients who are unable to maintain oral intake, have a coagulopathy (INR >1.5), have encephalopathy, or have pre-existing liver disease. Antiviral therapy is started in acute hepatitis B only in specific circumstances: severe or fulminant disease (INR >1.5 or bilirubin >10 mg/dL), immunosuppression, or anticipated need for immunosuppressive therapy. Oral tenofovir disoproxil fumarate 300 mg daily or entecavir 0.5 mg daily are used, continued until hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) clears and seroconversion to anti-HBs occurs. For acute hepatitis C, current AASLD/IDSA 2024 guidelines recommend direct-acting antiviral therapy (sofosbuvir-based regimens) if spontaneous clearance has not occurred within 12-16 weeks; early treatment achieves SVR12 rates above 95%. Ribavirin 600-1,000 mg/day has been used for severe HEV in immunocompromised patients based on case series data. Acute liver failure from any cause requires transfer to a transplant center, N-acetylcysteine infusion for hepatoprotection, and listing for emergency liver transplantation if the King's College criteria are met.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care (hydration, nutrition, rest)
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone of management for uncomplicated acute hepatitis A, B (mild-moderate), and E. Intravenous fluids and antiemetics when oral intake is inadequate. Avoidance of all hepatotoxic drugs and alcohol throughout.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: More than 99% of acute HAV and most acute HBV in immunocompetent adults resolve without specific antivirals

medicalTreatments.name: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate 300 mg orally daily
medicalTreatments.description: Nucleotide analog antiviral initiated in severe acute HBV (INR >1.5 or bilirubin >10 mg/dL) and in immunosuppressed patients. Continued until HBsAg clearance confirmed on two tests 6 months apart.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces HBV DNA to undetectable in 90-95% of treated patients within 24 weeks; promotes HBsAg seroconversion in severe acute disease

medicalTreatments.name: Entecavir 0.5 mg orally daily
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative nucleoside analog for severe acute hepatitis B with similar efficacy to tenofovir; preferred in patients with renal impairment (dose adjustment required).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HBV DNA undetectable in 90%+ by week 48; equivalent to tenofovir in randomized comparisons

medicalTreatments.name: Direct-acting antivirals (sofosbuvir + daclatasvir or sofosbuvir/ledipasvir, 8-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Started at 12-16 weeks post-exposure if acute hepatitis C has not spontaneously cleared. Oral regimen, taken once daily with or without food.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained virological response (SVR12) exceeding 95% in acute HCV; cure prevents transition to chronic infection and eliminates downstream cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma risk

medicalTreatments.name: N-acetylcysteine infusion (72-hour protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous hepatoprotectant that replenishes hepatic glutathione stores; used in all causes of acute liver failure, not just acetaminophen toxicity. Given as soon as acute liver failure (encephalopathy + coagulopathy) is recognized.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2014 review: improves transplant-free survival in non-acetaminophen acute liver failure by approximately 15%; does not replace transplantation in severe cases

medicalTreatments.name: Ribavirin 600-1,000 mg/day orally
medicalTreatments.description: Used in HEV-related hepatitis in immunocompromised patients (organ transplant recipients, HIV) where infection may become chronic. Data primarily from case series rather than randomized trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Viral clearance in 70-80% of organ-transplant recipients with chronic HEV based on case cohorts; duration typically 3-6 months

surgicalOptions.name: Emergency liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: The only definitive treatment for acute liver failure meeting King's College Criteria (pH <7.25, INR >6.5, creatinine >3.4 mg/dL, grade III-IV encephalopathy). Survival post-transplant is 60-80% at 1 year for acute viral hepatitis-associated failure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 1-year post-transplant survival 60-80%; 5-year survival 55-70% in viral hepatitis-associated acute liver failure

alternativeTreatments.name: Silymarin (milk thistle extract)
alternativeTreatments.description: A flavonoid complex with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties used in some European centers for acute hepatitis; some small randomized trials show modest transaminase reduction in toxic and viral hepatitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Glycyrrhizin (intravenous)
alternativeTreatments.description: An extract of licorice root used in Japan and parts of Asia for viral hepatitis; reduces ALT levels modestly in controlled trials but no effect on viral clearance or clinical outcomes has been demonstrated.

whySeeSpecialist: A hepatologist or gastroenterologist should evaluate any patient with acute viral hepatitis not resolving within 6 weeks, a coagulopathy, encephalopathy, or clinical features suggesting pre-existing liver disease. An infectious disease specialist guides treatment of acute hepatitis C and complex HBV co-infection scenarios.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hepatologist or gastroenterologist with a liver unit for moderate-to-severe disease; for milder presentations, a generalist or infectious disease physician can manage the acute episode with follow-up liver function tests. If acute liver failure develops, transfer to a liver transplant center is essential regardless of etiology.
hospitalCriteria: Inability to maintain oral intake due to severe nausea and vomiting
Coagulopathy (INR >1.5) or thrombocytopenia raising hemorrhage risk
Hepatic encephalopathy (any grade) — requires transplant center contact immediately
Hypoglycemia or electrolyte abnormalities requiring intravenous correction
Severe acute hepatitis in pregnancy (hepatitis E third trimester)
keyFacilities: Liver transplant centers with 24-hour listing capability for acute liver failure
Hepatology inpatient units with coagulation monitoring
Viral hepatitis outpatient clinics for uncomplicated acute cases and follow-up
Infectious disease services for HBV/HCV co-infection management
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate against hepatitis A with 2-dose inactivated vaccine series (HAV-B or monovalent); provides greater than 95% protection lasting 10-20+ years.
Vaccinate against hepatitis B with 3-dose or 2-dose series; all infants should receive the first dose within 24 hours of birth plus immunoglobulin if mother is HBsAg-positive.
Screen all blood donors, organ donors, and blood products for HBV, HCV, and HDV; universal blood safety screening has eliminated post-transfusion hepatitis C in countries with robust health systems.
Use sterile needles and equipment for all injections; harm-reduction needle exchange programs reduce HCV transmission by 40-60%.
Practice safe food and water precautions in hepatitis A and E endemic areas — boil water, avoid raw shellfish, and eat well-cooked produce.
Screen all pregnant women for HBsAg and provide neonates born to positive mothers with hepatitis B vaccine plus hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth.
lifestyleModifications: Abstain completely from alcohol throughout the acute illness and for at least 6 months after transaminase normalization; alcohol markedly worsens liver injury and delays recovery.
Stop all non-essential medications including acetaminophen, NSAIDs, herbal supplements, and recreational drugs without medical advice.
Maintain adequate fluid intake (2-3 L/day) and small, frequent, low-fat meals to reduce nausea and support liver metabolism.
Rest from work or school during symptomatic phase; most patients with uncomplicated acute hepatitis can return to normal activity once jaundice resolves and fatigue improves.
Notify all sexual partners and household contacts for hepatitis B or C serology and vaccination if not already immune.
Abstain from unprotected sexual intercourse until serology confirms that HBV or HCV has cleared or is not present in the partner.

recommended: Small frequent low-fat meals — fatty foods worsen nausea and impair biliary flow during cholestatic hepatitis
Adequate carbohydrates (rice, bread, pasta) as the liver's primary fuel substrate and to prevent hypoglycemia
Protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day from lean sources to support hepatocyte regeneration; does not need restriction in acute hepatitis without encephalopathy
High-antioxidant fruits and vegetables (berries, citrus, leafy greens) to support glutathione synthesis
avoid: All alcohol, which is directly hepatotoxic and synergistically worsens viral hepatitis damage
Raw shellfish (oysters, clams, mussels) which can harbor HAV and HEV and other pathogens harmful to a compromised liver
High-dose acetaminophen (above 2 g/day) and NSAIDs which reduce hepatic glutathione and may precipitate liver failure
Herbal supplements with known hepatotoxicity (kava, comfrey, pennyroyal, greater celandine)

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is advised during the symptomatic phase when fatigue and jaundice are present. Light walking is permitted as tolerated once acute symptoms begin to resolve. Contact sports, heavy manual labor, and vigorous aerobic exercise should be avoided until transaminases normalize. Patients with acute liver failure or coagulopathy must remain at complete rest in hospital.
dailyManagement: Check urine and stool color daily and report worsening jaundice, encephalopathy symptoms (confusion, hand tremor), or new abdominal distension immediately.
Take only medications approved by the treating physician — many common drugs including paracetamol, NSAIDs, and statins are hepatotoxic at standard doses in acute hepatitis.
Monitor weight weekly; loss of more than 2 kg/week may indicate poor nutritional intake requiring supplementation or intravenous support.
Follow hepatitis B or C status with serial blood tests at 4, 12, and 24 weeks to confirm clearance or identify chronicity early.
Notify household and sexual contacts to be tested and vaccinated as appropriate.
prognosis: Prognosis varies substantially by viral type. Hepatitis A resolves completely in over 99% of immunocompetent adults within 8 weeks; fulminant failure risk is below 0.5% (rising to 2% in patients over 50 or those with underlying liver disease). Acute hepatitis B in immunocompetent adults resolves in 90-95%, with chronicity in 5-10%; fulminant disease in 0.5-1%. The 5-year risk of cirrhosis in untreated chronic HBV is 8-20%. Acute hepatitis C becomes chronic in 55-85% of untreated cases; chronic HCV leads to cirrhosis in 15-30% over 20 years and hepatocellular carcinoma in 1-3% per year thereafter. Direct-acting antivirals cure more than 95% of chronic HCV once detected. Hepatitis E is self-limiting in immunocompetent adults with less than 1% mortality; in pregnant women (third trimester), mortality rises to 15-25%. Acute liver failure from any viral hepatitis has mortality above 50% without transplantation; post-transplant 1-year survival is 60-80%.
recoveryTimeline: Hepatitis A: symptoms resolve in 4-8 weeks; fatigue may persist for 3-6 months. Hepatitis B (acute): jaundice clears 4-12 weeks; HBsAg clearance typically at 3-6 months. Hepatitis C: if not treated, spontaneous clearance occurs within 6 months in 15-45% of cases; those who do not clear need antiviral therapy. Hepatitis E: recovery in 4-8 weeks in immunocompetent adults.
complications: Acute liver failure with hepatic encephalopathy and coagulopathy — rare (under 1% in HAV, 0.5-1% in HBV) but carries greater than 50% mortality without liver transplantation.
Chronic hepatitis B or C from failure to clear acute infection — establishes a decades-long pathway to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
Cholestatic hepatitis A — prolonged jaundice and pruritus lasting 3-6 months in 5-10% of HAV cases, almost always resolving spontaneously.
Relapsing hepatitis A — biphasic course with second transaminase peak 4-12 weeks after apparent recovery in up to 12% of cases; full resolution is eventual.
Hepatocellular carcinoma — a long-term complication of untreated or incompletely treated chronic HBV and HCV; 6-monthly ultrasound surveillance reduces late-stage detection.
Cryoglobulinemia and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis — extrahepatic complications of chronic HCV infection causing purpura, arthralgia, and renal disease.

supportResources.name: WHO Hepatitis Information
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, vaccine recommendations, and treatment guidelines for all hepatitis viruses.

supportResources.name: American Liver Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient education, support groups, and research funding for liver diseases including viral hepatitis.

supportResources.name: CDC Viral Hepatitis
supportResources.description: US surveillance data, vaccination schedules, prevention strategies, and testing guidelines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute hepatitis B is characterized by anti-HBc IgM positivity and HBsAg appearing within 6 months of exposure with subsequent clearance in 90-95% of immunocompetent adults. Chronic hepatitis B is defined by HBsAg persistence for more than 6 months with absent anti-HBc IgM; it requires long-term antiviral therapy and surveillance rather than the supportive acute-phase management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Hepatitis C
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute hepatitis C typically presents within 2-24 weeks of exposure with or without symptoms and a positive HCV RNA before anti-HCV antibody appears. Chronic HCV (persistence beyond 6 months) has a positive anti-HCV antibody and requires direct-acting antiviral treatment; acute HCV may clear spontaneously in 15-45% if observed, influencing the timing of therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Yellow Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Yellow fever virus causes acute viral hepatitis with jaundice and coagulopathy in severe cases but is restricted to sub-Saharan Africa and tropical South America, is transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, presents with prominent hemorrhagic features and renal failure, and is confirmed by anti-YFV IgM serology. Standard viral hepatitis serology is negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis (Weil disease) causes jaundice, acute kidney injury, and uveitis following freshwater exposure in tropical regions. Unlike viral hepatitis, it presents with high fever, myalgia (especially calf pain), conjunctival suffusion, and renal involvement; diagnosis is by serology (MAT) or culture, and treatment is with penicillin or doxycycline.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: The chronic form of HBV infection that persists after acute hepatitis B fails to resolve; the single largest contributor to hepatocellular carcinoma worldwide.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic HCV is the most common cause of liver transplantation in high-income countries; shares bloodborne transmission with HBV and responds to direct-acting antivirals.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Arboviral hemorrhagic fever with acute hepatic involvement; shares jaundice as a cardinal feature and is prevented by the same vaccine used for travel to endemic regions alongside hepatitis A vaccination.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Spirochetal infection causing jaundice and liver dysfunction that enters the differential diagnosis for acute viral hepatitis in travelers and those with freshwater exposure.

faqs.question: What is acute viral hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Acute viral hepatitis is sudden-onset liver inflammation caused by one of five viruses (A, B, C, D, or E). The virus infects hepatocytes, triggering an immune response that destroys infected liver cells and raises blood enzyme levels. Most cases cause jaundice, fatigue, and nausea; most resolve within weeks, though hepatitis B and C can become chronic.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of acute hepatitis?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptoms appear 1-2 weeks before jaundice: profound fatigue, loss of appetite, nausea, and dark urine (bilirubinuria). Smokers often notice a sudden aversion to cigarettes. Jaundice and pale stools develop later as bilirubin rises. Low-grade fever and joint aches precede hepatitis B jaundice in some patients.

faqs.question: How long does acute viral hepatitis last?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A and E in healthy adults resolve within 4-8 weeks; fatigue may persist for 3-6 months. Acute hepatitis B clears in 90-95% of immunocompetent adults within 6 months. Hepatitis C becomes chronic (lasting more than 6 months) in 55-85% of those infected if not treated.

faqs.question: Does acute hepatitis require hospitalization?
faqs.answer: Most cases can be managed at home with rest and oral hydration. Hospitalization is needed for patients unable to drink fluids, those with INR above 1.5 or encephalopathy (signs of liver failure), pregnant women with hepatitis E, or anyone with underlying liver disease who develops acute hepatitis.

faqs.question: Which hepatitis virus can become chronic?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis B becomes chronic in 5-10% of immunocompetent adults and up to 90% of neonates infected at birth. Hepatitis C becomes chronic in 55-85% of infected individuals. Hepatitis D is chronic in 70-90% of HBV superinfections. Hepatitis A and E (in immunocompetent adults) do not become chronic.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for acute viral hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A and E in healthy adults are treated supportively (rest, fluids, no alcohol or hepatotoxic drugs). Severe acute hepatitis B is treated with tenofovir or entecavir. Acute hepatitis C that has not cleared by 12-16 weeks is treated with direct-acting antivirals achieving cure rates above 95%.

faqs.question: Can you get acute hepatitis from food?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A and E are spread by contaminated food and water. Raw or undercooked shellfish harvested from sewage-contaminated water is a common hepatitis A source. Hepatitis E genotype 3 and 4 are transmitted via undercooked pork and wild boar. Hepatitis B, C, and D are not spread through food.

faqs.question: What blood tests confirm acute hepatitis?
faqs.answer: A liver panel shows markedly elevated ALT and AST (10-100 times normal). Anti-HAV IgM confirms acute hepatitis A. Anti-HBc IgM plus HBsAg confirms acute hepatitis B. HCV RNA PCR confirms acute hepatitis C (antibody may be negative early). Anti-HEV IgM confirms hepatitis E.

faqs.question: How is acute hepatitis transmitted?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A and E spread via contaminated food and water (fecal-oral). Hepatitis B spreads via blood, unprotected sex, and mother-to-child at birth. Hepatitis C spreads mainly through shared needles and blood exposure. Hepatitis D requires simultaneous hepatitis B infection and spreads via the same routes.

faqs.question: Is acute hepatitis dangerous in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis E genotype 1 in the third trimester of pregnancy causes fulminant liver failure in 15-25% of women, with maternal mortality from coagulopathy and encephalopathy. Hepatitis B in pregnancy can transmit to the neonate at delivery; immediate vaccination and immunoglobulin (within 12 hours of birth) reduces neonatal risk by over 90%.

faqs.question: Can acute hepatitis cause liver failure?
faqs.answer: Acute liver failure occurs in under 1% of hepatitis A and 0.5-1% of acute hepatitis B cases in healthy adults. Risk is much higher in those with pre-existing liver disease. Signs of liver failure include confusion, hand tremor, worsening jaundice, and easy bruising. Emergency liver transplantation may be required.

faqs.question: Can you prevent hepatitis A and B with vaccines?
faqs.answer: Yes. The hepatitis A vaccine (2 doses) prevents more than 95% of infections and is recommended for all travelers to endemic areas and children in routine schedules. The hepatitis B vaccine (3 doses) prevents more than 90% of infections and is recommended universally in infancy and for unvaccinated adolescents and at-risk adults.

faqs.question: What should you avoid eating with acute hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Avoid all alcohol, which worsens liver damage and slows recovery. Avoid raw shellfish due to risk of superimposed infection. Avoid high-dose acetaminophen (above 2 g/day), NSAIDs, and herbal supplements with hepatotoxic potential. Fatty meals worsen nausea; small frequent low-fat meals are better tolerated.

faqs.question: How long are you contagious with acute hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis A: most contagious 2 weeks before jaundice appears; contagiousness drops sharply once jaundice starts. Hepatitis B and C: contagious as long as the virus is detectable in blood. Strict hand hygiene (hepatitis A/E) and universal precautions for blood (hepatitis B/C) are appropriate throughout the illness.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic hepatitis?
faqs.answer: Acute hepatitis is liver inflammation lasting less than 6 months; most cases caused by HAV or HEV are acute only. Chronic hepatitis persists beyond 6 months and is defined by ongoing viral replication or liver inflammation. Chronic HBV and HCV carry long-term risks of cirrhosis and liver cancer that acute infection alone does not.

simpleName: Acute hepatitis (sudden liver infection from a virus)
metaTitle: Acute Viral Hepatitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
metaDescription: Acute viral hepatitis: sudden liver inflammation from hepatitis A, B, C, D, or E. Symptoms, jaundice, diagnosis, antiviral treatment, and prognosis reviewed.
